title: EoE Swing Symbolism and Trains too
display_title: EoE Swing Symbolism and Trains too
wikitext:
[]: EoE Swing Symbolism (and Trains, too)             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Feb 15, 2007 5:03 pm What's the deal with that swing we see several times throughout EoE? Is it symbolism of Shinji wavering between not knowing weather to run away or accept reality, similar to the symbolism of SDAT Tracks 25 and 26?? And in terms of the mental train (16, 20, 26', probably some other episodes I'm forgetting) is it just meant as that "Train as a jounery" symbol, nothing in terms of his inner space/his mind?

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Thu Feb 15, 2007 5:14 pm I think Freud said, "Sometimes a swing is just a swing." Or something like that.

[]:             Quote Post by drinian » Thu Feb 15, 2007 5:16 pm Someone on the EoE English commentary track made the comment that it's very similar to some of Pink Floyd's imagery. Insightful? Maybe.

[]:             Quote Post by TriLink » Thu Feb 15, 2007 5:52 pm I think it was Anno's way of trying to illustrate how lonely Shinji was. Most people have someone pushing them on the swing, but he was on it by himself. Just another way of trying to get audience to feel sorry for him in my opinion. And for the train, my guess is that it was meant to symbolize him running away from his problems (like when he ran away from Misato's at the start of the show). When Shinji is upset, he tends to run away from his problems rather than face them head on, So what better way to symbolzie that than with a train, something that's constantly in motion, never in the same place for too long.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Thu Feb 15, 2007 9:46 pm I personally thought the swing, and especially the sandbox scene, was a flashback sequence. As for the train, I thought it was a symbol for Shinji's psyche in a confused state, where he should go, what he should do, cue the immortal line : Ikari Shinji-kun wrote: I mustn't run away...I mustn't run away...I musn't run away...

[]:             Quote Post by Tokpile Quohog » Fri Feb 16, 2007 2:49 pm I see the train as Shinji's futile attempt at running away. Trains are predestined to where they will and will eventually come back to the beginning. Shinji tries running away but just gets sucked back in, or something like that. What would I know? For the swing, I also find that image very "floydian".

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Fri Feb 16, 2007 3:20 pm Jabberwok had this to say about the swing on this thread over at ANF Jabberwok wrote: I was thinking about what the swing set may represent and I had the notion that the motion of the swing may be tied to Shinji's emotional state throughout the narrative suggested by Ornette earlier. I was thinking it may represent a manic/depressive cycle or, at the very least, an emotional barometer or sorts. When I rewatched it, I found some neat correlations... At the beginning, the swing is in motion, it's pace is steady. Shinji makes his remark about coming here and finding something. When the camera shifts, the rusty creaking can still be heard. The swing at this point represents a neutral emotional state?

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Fri Feb 16, 2007 8:46 pm I has some thoughts earlier about the symbolism of the train which I'd like to elaborate on. The consensus seems to be that the train represents Shinji's own "private hell", where all his demons come to haunt him. Here are some relevant images of the "private hell" carraige.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri Feb 16, 2007 9:26 pm I like the train idea. But, regarding #16, why do you think that Shinji is interacting with Leliel, as opposed to just "talking to himself"? Clearly the other Rei encountered in #23 is Armisael, and I've considered that Arael appears briefly to Asuka ("Are you lonely?" ... "Liar.") -- but, here?

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Fri Feb 16, 2007 10:04 pm Reichu wrote: But, regarding #16, why do you think that Shinji is interacting with Leliel, as opposed to just "talking to himself"? Clearly the other Rei encountered in #23 is Armisael, and I've considered that Arael appears briefly to Asuka ("Are you lonely?" ... "Liar.") -- but, here?

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Fri Feb 16, 2007 10:26 pm Can it really be considered Shinji's private hell? If Leliel was talking to him then none of the instances were in any way private seeing as other people are present. Especially the first and third ones since they involve people seeing his memories. Personal hell might be a far more fitting description. LOL Wrong Angel

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri Feb 16, 2007 10:26 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: Firstly, an interview with one of the production staff that The Evamonkey should be able to reference where the idea that Shinji is communicating with the angel is explicitly mentioned.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sat Feb 17, 2007 8:18 pm @ Reichu: I like the idea of Arael talking to Asuka as opposed it being lil Asuka. @ ObsessiveMathsFreak: FTW due to the idea of the train being his personal hell. I didn't think episode 4 was a result of the train motif, but it's possible that ride still had an impact on him. *shrugs* LOLZ @ EOTV/EOE FORESHADOWING wrote: The self which is actually seen, and the self observing that. There are many entities called Shinji Ikari. The other Shinji Ikari that exists in your mind. The Shinji Ikari in Misato Katsuragi's mind, the Shinji in Asuka Sohryu, the Shinji in Rei Ayanami, and the Shinji in Gendo Ikari. All are different Shinji Ikaris, but each of them is a true Shinji Ikari. You are afraid of those Shinji Ikaris in other people's minds

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Feb 17, 2007 8:46 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: @ Reichu: I like the idea of Arael talking to Asuka as opposed it being lil Asuka.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sat Feb 17, 2007 8:58 pm @ Reichu: My last post edited at your request. ;) Hmmm...Never thought about the parallels before between 22' and 23'. Very good point with that, though. Though one thing I'm wondering about is why do Angels 15-17 contact Asuka, Rei, and Shinji? Trying to let the Three Children know that the Angels aren't the enemy; that they only want to return to their Mother? Reichu wrote: Re: the train stuff in #22' -- hmm, well, those need to be taken into consideration, don't they?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Feb 17, 2007 9:23 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: Though one thing I'm wondering about is why do Angels 15-17 contact Asuka, Rei, and Shinji? Trying to let the Three Children know that the Angels aren't the enemy; that they only want to return to their Mother?

[]:             Quote Post by C4 » Sun Feb 18, 2007 1:29 am I always figured that the swing was a symbol of Shinji's lost childhood. I'm almost 100 percent sure that you never see anybody swinging on it, and I think that's because Shinji really never had a chance to be that type of happy kid on the playground to be pushed around on a swing without any worries. He lost his mother and more or less, his father at a very young age, so even if he was able to find a semi-normal childhood living with his teacher, he would still remember his losses, which prevent him from ever being carefree for an extended period of time. It's just a sad, lonely swing, swinging back and forth with no one to push, and no one to ride it. As for the train, I thought it worked on a couple of levels: the first of which builds on the loneliness issue. An empty train, at least to me, always seemed to be an extremely lonely place, much more so than say, a large room, or an empty house. Especially in Japan, where trains are very often crowded, to have no one on a train is just an unsettling feeling. To ride on a train alone, you would have to sit amongst dozens of empty seats; seats where people are supposed to be.(I realize that trains aren't always going to be at full capacity, but the fact is, a seat was constructed for someone to sit in.) Perhaps worse than being on a train alone is being on a train with only one or two other people. If two people are alone on a train, their attentions, if only a small part, will be invariably directed towards the other person. More often than not, two people alone on a train just sit in silence, and this silence creates a tension, and brings to the forefront the natural barriers that everybody has. And even if two people are on a train alone and happen to know each other(I'm going on my experiences now;) conversations on a train are just a little bit awkward, perhaps because they're filling up a large space, or perhaps because there is the overall feeling that if you're on a train ride with someone you know, than you need to talk to them, creating a bit of underlying pressure on the situation. And I always figured that the train was always moving in Shinji's mind segments because it was representative of his mental/emotional unstableness. It was always moving, never calm, or at peace. So basically, I'm a fan of the manifestation of Shinji's need to run away theory. As for the angels contacting the pilots, my take on it was that they were not contacting them in the sense that they were trying to communicate with them, but that they were forcing the pilots to communicate with themselves.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Feb 18, 2007 7:16 am Yay! My first post on this Eva forum! Okay, the train has been covered a lot, and I like a lot of the ideas about its representation (running away, Shinji's personal hell, and his earliest memory of pain all fits very nicely). The swing I think is far more perplexing because it only shows up the one time. But I think that's one of the most fascinating scenes in all of NGE. It's so serene and surreal. I love the way it's set up, everything is perfectly symmetrical. The whole scene down to the rails next to the swing reminds of those swinging ball thingies people buy and put on their desks. Then we have the background, the mountains on the side of the swing, the sun set perfectly in the middle, and the swing perfectly centered between them. I think there has to be some symbolism to this scene and I've been wondering what it could all mean. My first thought is that the sun/moon in the series seems to symbolize the end and the beginning. Birth and death. In fact, I think you see them associated with Rei several times in the series (I'm relatively new to NGE, so maybe the more experienced fan-geeks can give me some references here - to the sun especially). Then, we have young Shinji building a replica of Nerv in a sandbox. I won't go into all my thoughts about the symbolism of this scene (there's a lot quite frankly, and a lot of it's just speculation), but I will point out what I think is the most obvious: When Shinji's building the sand castle, we see the swing swinging easily back and forth and the sun up over the mountains. When Shinji starts to destroy it, we see the the swing start to stop, eclipsing the now setting sun. What this seems to symbolize to me is creation from life to death. The swing and sun are symbols of life, and Shinji is acting this out in the foreground. But that's just my interpretation. Sorry for the length, it happens to be a favorite scene of mine. :oops:

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Feb 18, 2007 9:14 pm @ Eva Yojimbo: Welcome! I like what you have to say about the swing, but personally I don't know what else it can mean besides the input I already gave. btw, we see that swing in the P3II sand castle scene, the "do you want to become one with me?" woman's voices scene, and the "dream" Shinji had as a subsitute for reality. @ C4: Interesting how you think the Angels want the Children to commuincate with themselves. I never thought of it that way, but it makes a whole lot of sense. Interesting how you think the train is symbolism how mentally he's never calm, that's a really good point. Of course, I agree it shows his wanting to run away. ;)

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Feb 18, 2007 10:37 pm C4 wrote: As for the angels contacting the pilots, my take on it was that they were not contacting them in the sense that they were trying to communicate with them, but that they were forcing the pilots to communicate with themselves.
